cards.module_title: Orientation and Compliance Boundaries
cards.display_title: Welcome and Course Purpose - Challenge Debrief
cards.learner_facing_content:
Challenge Debrief - lesson re-teaching extension. Your response has been submitted.

Safety Principle: When you are unsure, choose the action that protects the resident first, stays inside CNA scope, and keeps required observe-report-document and care-plan steps in the right order.

Safest Response: 48
Why This Is Safest: California requires 48 hours over the two-year renewal period.

CNA Scope Note: As a CNA you observe, report, document, and follow the care plan. You do not diagnose, change orders, or perform tasks outside your training and assignment.
Resident Safety Note: Resident safety and dignity come first. Prevent avoidable harm, protect privacy, and report any change of condition promptly to the licensed nurse.

What to Remember: Lead with the action that keeps the resident safest, then complete the remaining observe-report-document steps in order. California requires 48 hours over the two-year renewal period.
cards.learning_goal: Explain the decision and apply non-punitive remediation.
cards.cna_practice_example: After a missed practice decision, review the key safety step and repeat the scenario without penalty.
why_it_matters: Supports safe CNA judgment within scope of practice.
Builds audit-ready completion evidence for required online theory only.
Preserves compliance boundaries: no PHI, no clinical-hour credit, certificate production disabled.

key_terms.definition: The learner observes, reports, documents, and follows the care plan within allowed CNA duties.

cards.completion_condition: Submit a response, then review the safest response and your own choice before completing the lesson.
cards.narration_script: Challenge debrief. Your response has been submitted. When you are unsure, choose the action that protects the resident first, stays inside CNA scope, and keeps required observe-report-document and care-plan steps in the right order. The safest response is to 48. California requires 48 hours over the two-year renewal period. As a CNA, stay within scope: observe, report, document, and follow the care plan. Resident safety and dignity come first, so report any change of condition promptly to the nurse. Before you continue, review the safest response and your own choice so the reason stays with you on the floor.

cards.display_title: Acknowledgement and Compliance Check - Challenge Debrief
cards.learner_facing_content:
Challenge Debrief - lesson re-teaching extension. Your response has been submitted.

Safety Principle: When you are unsure, choose the action that protects the resident first, stays inside CNA scope, and keeps required observe-report-document and care-plan steps in the right order.

Safest Response: 48
Why This Is Safest: California requires 48 hours over the two-year renewal period.

CNA Scope Note: As a CNA you observe, report, document, and follow the care plan. You do not diagnose, change orders, or perform tasks outside your training and assignment.
Resident Safety Note: Resident safety and dignity come first. Prevent avoidable harm, protect privacy, and report any change of condition promptly to the licensed nurse.

What to Remember: Lead with the action that keeps the resident safest, then complete the remaining observe-report-document steps in order. California requires 48 hours over the two-year renewal period.

cards.module_title: Infection Control and PPE
cards.lesson_title: Why Infection Control Matters in Long-Term Care
cards.learner_facing_content: A healthcare-associated infection, or HAI, is an infection a person gets while receiving care in a healthcare facility. HAIs are a serious concern in long-term care because residents often have weakened immune systems due to age or chronic illness, residents live in close proximity to one another, many residents need hands-on assistance with daily care, and shared equipment and common areas create opportunities for germ transmission. As a CNA, you have direct contact with residents throughout your shift. That places you in a unique position to both prevent infections and recognize them early.
cards.learning_goal: Teach: Healthcare-Associated Infections in Long-Term Care.
cards.cna_practice_example: Apply this during routine CNA care while staying within scope: observe, protect, and report.
why_it_matters: Supports safe CNA judgment within scope of practice.
Builds audit-ready completion evidence for required online theory only.
Preserves compliance boundaries: no PHI, no clinical-hour credit, certificate production disabled.

key_terms.definition: An infection a person acquires while receiving care in a healthcare facility.

cards.module_title: Infection Control and PPE
cards.lesson_title: Why Infection Control Matters in Long-Term Care
cards.display_title: Why Long-Term Care Carries Unique Infection Risk
cards.learner_facing_content: Long-term care residents face unique infection risks. Advanced age weakens the immune response. Chronic conditions such as diabetes, COPD, and heart failure reduce resistance. Indwelling devices like catheters and feeding tubes create entry points for germs. Shared living spaces such as dining rooms, activity areas, and bathrooms are used by many people. Residents with cognitive impairment may not be able to follow hygiene steps, and multiple caregivers mean each contact is a potential transmission point. Knowing these risks helps you stay alert during routine care.

cards.module_title: Infection Control and PPE
cards.lesson_title: Why Infection Control Matters in Long-Term Care
cards.display_title: Why Infection Control Matters in Long-Term Care - Challenge Debrief
cards.learner_facing_content:
Challenge Debrief - lesson re-teaching extension. Your response has been submitted.

Safety Principle: Infection prevention starts with the action that breaks the chain of transmission for every resident, not just the ones who look sick. Hand hygiene, done correctly and at the right moments, is that action.

Safest Response: Hand hygiene performed correctly and at the right times
Why This Is Safest: Hand hygiene performed correctly and at the right times is the single most effective way to prevent the spread of infection in long-term care. Hands are the main way germs travel between residents, surfaces, and you, so clean hands before and after every contact stop transmission at its most common point.

CNA Scope Note: Performing hand hygiene at the right moments is squarely within CNA scope. You are expected to clean your hands before and after resident contact, after removing gloves, and after contact with body fluids or contaminated surfaces.
Resident Safety Note: Every resident is protected when you clean your hands at the right times. This is also how you protect yourself, your coworkers, and residents who cannot fight infection well.

What to Remember: Gloves, masks, and isolation all have a place, but none of them replace hand hygiene. Clean hands at the right moments are your strongest, most reliable infection-control tool on every shift.

remediation.safety_principle: Infection prevention starts with the action that breaks the chain of transmission for every resident, not just the ones who look sick. Hand hygiene, done correctly and at the right moments, is that action.

safest_response.text: Hand hygiene performed correctly and at the right times

remediation.why_correct: Hand hygiene performed correctly and at the right times is the single most effective way to prevent the spread of infection in long-term care. Hands are the main way germs travel between residents, surfaces, and you, so clean hands before and after every contact stop transmission at its most common point.

why_incorrect.A: Gloves are useful, but wearing them for all contact does not replace hand hygiene. Germs reach the skin when gloves are removed or reused, and gloves give a false sense of safety if hands are not cleaned before and after.
why_incorrect.C: A surgical mask protects against certain droplet exposures but does nothing about germs carried on the hands, which cause most transmission in long-term care. Routine masking for all care is not the most effective general measure.
why_incorrect.D: Isolating residents with known infections is only part of the picture. Many residents carry germs without obvious signs, so relying on isolation alone misses the everyday transmission that hand hygiene prevents.

option_rationales.label: Wearing gloves during all resident contact
option_rationales.why: Gloves are useful, but wearing them for all contact does not replace hand hygiene. Germs reach the skin when gloves are removed or reused, and gloves give a false sense of safety if hands are not cleaned before and after.
option_rationales.remember: Always perform hand hygiene before applying and after removing gloves - gloves are an addition to clean hands, not a substitute.

option_rationales.label: Hand hygiene performed correctly and at the right times
option_rationales.why: Hand hygiene performed correctly and at the right times is the single most effective measure for preventing the spread of infection, because hands are the most common way germs move between residents and surfaces.
option_rationales.remember: Make correct, well-timed hand hygiene your automatic first infection-control habit on every shift.

option_rationales.label: Using a surgical mask during all care
option_rationales.why: A surgical mask protects against certain droplet exposures but does nothing about germs carried on the hands, which cause most transmission in long-term care. Routine masking for all care is not the most effective general measure.
option_rationales.remember: Use masks when the situation or precautions call for them - they support, but never replace, hand hygiene.

option_rationales.label: Isolating all residents with known infections
option_rationales.why: Isolating residents with known infections is only part of the picture. Many residents carry germs without obvious signs, so relying on isolation alone misses the everyday transmission that hand hygiene prevents.
option_rationales.remember: Standard precautions and hand hygiene apply to every resident, not only those already known to be infected.

remediation.resident_safety_note: Every resident is protected when you clean your hands at the right times. This is also how you protect yourself, your coworkers, and residents who cannot fight infection well.
remediation.cna_scope_note: Performing hand hygiene at the right moments is squarely within CNA scope. You are expected to clean your hands before and after resident contact, after removing gloves, and after contact with body fluids or contaminated surfaces.
remediation.remember_this: Gloves, masks, and isolation all have a place, but none of them replace hand hygiene. Clean hands at the right moments are your strongest, most reliable infection-control tool on every shift.

cards.module_title: Infection Control and PPE
cards.display_title: The Six Links in the Chain of Infection
cards.learner_facing_content: Infections spread through a chain of six connected links. Break any one link and you can stop the infection from spreading. The links are: the infectious agent, the germ such as a bacterium, virus, or fungus that causes disease; the reservoir, where the germ lives and multiplies, such as a person, a contaminated surface, or standing water; the portal of exit, how the germ leaves the reservoir through body fluids, respiratory droplets, or skin contact; the mode of transmission, how the germ travels to a new host by direct contact, droplet, airborne spread, or contaminated objects; the portal of entry, how the germ enters a new host through breaks in skin, mucous membranes, or the respiratory tract; and the susceptible host, a person whose body cannot fight off the germ.
cards.learning_goal: Teach: The Six Links in the Chain of Infection.
cards.cna_practice_example: Apply this during routine CNA care while staying within scope: observe, protect, and report.
why_it_matters: Supports safe CNA judgment within scope of practice.
Builds audit-ready completion evidence for required online theory only.
Preserves compliance boundaries: no PHI, no clinical-hour credit, certificate production disabled.

key_terms.definition: How a germ travels to a new host - direct contact, droplet, airborne, or contaminated objects.
A person whose body cannot easily fight off an infectious agent.

cards.module_title: Infection Control and PPE
cards.display_title: How CNAs Break the Chain Every Day
cards.learner_facing_content: As a CNA you break the chain every day. You attack the infectious agent through proper cleaning and disinfection. You reduce the reservoir with environmental cleaning and proper waste disposal. You close the portal of exit by covering wounds and handling linen properly. You block the mode of transmission with hand hygiene, PPE, and standard precautions. You protect the portal of entry through skin care and wound protection. And you support the susceptible host with good nutrition, mobility support, and early reporting of changes. Hand hygiene breaks the chain at the most common transmission point and is the single most effective way to prevent infection spread.

You block the mode of transmission with hand hygiene, PPE, and standard precautions. You protect the portal of entry through skin care and wound protection. And you support the susceptible host with good nutrition, mobility support, and early reporting of changes. Hand hygiene breaks the chain at the most common transmission point and is the single most effective way to prevent infection spread.

cards.display_title: The Chain of Infection - Challenge Debrief
cards.learner_facing_content:
Challenge Debrief - lesson re-teaching extension. Your response has been submitted.

Safety Principle: When you are unsure, choose the action that protects the resident first, stays inside CNA scope, and keeps required observe-report-document and care-plan steps in the right order.

Safest Response: Gloves then eye protection/face shield then gown then mask
Why This Is Safest: CDC/WHO-recommended doffing removes most contaminated items first (gloves) then eye protection then gown then mask.

CNA Scope Note: As a CNA you observe, report, document, and follow the care plan. You do not diagnose, change orders, or perform tasks outside your training and assignment.
Resident Safety Note: Resident safety and dignity come first. Prevent avoidable harm, protect privacy, and report any change of condition promptly to the licensed nurse.

What to Remember: Lead with the action that keeps the resident safest, then complete the remaining observe-report-document steps in order. CDC/WHO-recommended doffing removes most contaminated items first (gloves) then eye protection then gown then mask.

cards.module_title: Infection Control and PPE
cards.lesson_title: Hand Hygiene - Your Most Important Tool
cards.display_title: The WHO Five Moments for Hand Hygiene
cards.learner_facing_content: The World Health Organization identifies five key moments when healthcare workers must perform hand hygiene: before touching a resident; before a clean or aseptic procedure such as catheter care; after a body fluid exposure risk such as emptying a urinal; after touching a resident; and after touching a resident's surroundings such as the bed rail or call light. A helpful way to remember this is to picture a safety zone around each resident. Clean hands enter the zone, and clean hands leave the zone.
cards.learning_goal: Teach: The WHO Five Moments for Hand Hygiene.
cards.cna_practice_example: Apply this during routine CNA care while staying within scope: observe, protect, and report.
why_it_matters: Supports safe CNA judgment within scope of practice.
Builds audit-ready completion evidence for required online theory only.
Preserves compliance boundaries: no PHI, no clinical-hour credit, certificate production disabled.

key_terms.definition: Five required hand-hygiene moments: before touching, before clean procedure, after fluid risk, after touching, after surroundings.

cards.lesson_title: Hand Hygiene - Your Most Important Tool
cards.display_title: Soap and Water vs. Alcohol-Based Sanitizer
cards.learner_facing_content: Choose your method based on the situation. Use soap and water when hands are visibly soiled, after using the restroom, and after caring for a resident with C. difficile or norovirus; scrub for at least twenty seconds. Use alcohol-based hand sanitizer when hands are not visibly soiled and between routine resident contacts; cover all surfaces and rub until dry, about twenty seconds. Remember that alcohol-based sanitizer does not work against all germs, so use soap and water when hands are visibly dirty or after caring for residents with certain infections.

cards.module_title: Infection Control and PPE
cards.learner_facing_content: Standard precautions assume that all body fluids except sweat may contain infectious agents. That means you use protective measures for every resident, every time, not just for residents known to have an infection. Standard precautions include hand hygiene, PPE use based on the task, safe handling of sharps and contaminated items, proper linen handling, and respiratory hygiene and cough etiquette.
cards.learning_goal: Teach: Standard Precautions: Protect Every Time.

cards.module_title: Infection Control and PPE
cards.display_title: Selecting the Right PPE for the Task
cards.learner_facing_content: Match your PPE to the task. Routine vital signs usually need no PPE unless your skin may contact fluids. For bathing and perineal care, wear gloves, and add a gown if there is a splashing risk. When emptying a urinal or bedpan, wear gloves, and add a gown and eye protection if splashing is likely. Suctioning requires gloves, gown, mask, and eye protection. Changing soiled linen requires gloves, and a gown if it is saturated. For a resident on droplet precautions, wear gloves, gown, and a surgical mask, with eye protection per facility policy. When in doubt, protect more, not less.

cards.module_title: Infection Control and PPE
cards.display_title: Donning and Doffing PPE in the Correct Order
cards.learner_facing_content: Put PPE on in this order: gown, then mask, then eye protection, then gloves. Take PPE off in this order: gloves, then eye protection, then gown, then mask. The doffing sequence matters because gloves are the most contaminated item, so you remove them first to reduce the risk of touching your face or clothing with contaminated gloves. Always perform hand hygiene after removing all PPE.

cards.module_title: Infection Control and PPE
cards.display_title: Common Signs of Infection to Watch For
cards.learner_facing_content: Because you see residents more often than any other caregiver, you are often the first to notice changes. Report any of these signs to the licensed nurse: fever, or a temperature above the resident's normal range; a new or worsening cough; a change in sputum color, amount, or odor; a change in urine that is cloudy, foul-smelling, or dark; redness, swelling, warmth, or drainage around a wound or catheter site; a change in mental status such as new confusion, agitation, or lethargy, which in elderly residents may be the first sign of infection; decreased appetite or fluid intake; and new skin changes such as a rash, redness, or warmth.
cards.learning_goal: Teach: Common Signs of Infection to Watch For.
cards.cna_practice_example: Apply this during routine CNA care while staying within scope: observe, protect, and report.
why_it_matters: Supports safe CNA judgment within scope of practice.
Builds audit-ready completion evidence for required online theory only.
Preserves compliance boundaries: no PHI, no clinical-hour credit, certificate production disabled.

cards.module_title: Infection Control and PPE
cards.display_title: Reporting Infection Concerns: What, When, How
cards.learner_facing_content: Report new symptoms or changes from baseline immediately by telling the licensed nurse directly; do not wait for charting time. Report vital signs outside the normal range immediately, giving the numbers and describing what you observed. Report changes you noticed during care before the end of your shift at a minimum, following your facility's reporting process. Remember the boundary: you report, and the nurse assesses. Reporting a concern is never wrong, but failing to report one can be dangerous.

cards.learner_facing_content:
Scenario challenge for Recognizing and Reporting Infection Signs: When handling soiled linen, a CNA should:

A) Shake the linen to remove debris before bagging it
B) Hold the linen close to the body for stability
C) Hold the linen away from the body and place it directly in the linen bag
D) Place the linen on the floor temporarily

Why This Is Safest: Hold soiled linen away from the body and place it directly in the container; never shake it.

Resident Safety Note: Resident safety and dignity come first. Prevent avoidable harm, protect privacy, and report any change of condition promptly to the licensed nurse.

What to Remember: Lead with the action that keeps the resident safest, then complete the remaining observe-report-document steps in order. Hold soiled linen away from the body and place it directly in the container; never shake it.

cards.module_title: Infection Control and PPE
cards.learner_facing_content: A clean environment helps break the chain of infection at the reservoir link. Clean high-touch surfaces regularly, including bed rails, call lights, doorknobs, light switches, and toilet handles. Always clean from clean areas toward dirty areas and from top to bottom. Use facility-approved disinfectants and follow the contact-time instructions on the label. Never mix cleaning chemicals, and dispose of contaminated materials in the appropriate containers.
cards.learning_goal: Teach: Environmental Cleaning Principles.
cards.cna_practice_example: Apply this during routine CNA care while staying within scope: observe, protect, and report.
why_it_matters: Supports safe CNA judgment within scope of practice.

Preserves compliance boundaries: no PHI, no clinical-hour credit, certificate production disabled.

cards.module_title: Infection Control and PPE
cards.learner_facing_content: Handle soiled linen carefully to avoid spreading germs into the air or onto surfaces. Hold soiled linen away from your body. Do not shake soiled linen, because shaking can spread germs into the air. Place soiled linen in the designated linen bags or hampers immediately, and never place soiled linen on the floor. Wash your hands after handling soiled linen, even if you wore gloves.

cards.narration_script: Handle soiled linen carefully to avoid spreading germs into the air or onto surfaces. Hold soiled linen away from your body. Do not shake soiled linen, because shaking can spread germs into the air. Place soiled linen in the designated linen bags or hampers immediately, and never place soiled linen on the floor. Wash your hands after handling soiled linen, even if you wore gloves.

Wash your hands after handling soiled linen, even if you wore gloves.

cards.learner_facing_content: Handle sharps and waste with care. Never recap a used needle; if you encounter one, report it to the nurse. Dispose of sharps only in puncture-resistant sharps containers. Report any needlestick or sharps injury immediately. Follow your facility's biohazardous waste procedures for all contaminated items.

cards.display_title: Environmental Cleaning and Safe Practices Challenge
cards.learner_facing_content:
Scenario challenge for Environmental Cleaning and Safe Practices: The single most effective measure for preventing the spread of infection in a long-term care facility is:

A) Wearing gloves during all resident contact
B) Hand hygiene performed correctly and at the right times
C) Using a surgical mask during all care
D) Isolating all residents with known infections

No answer is revealed until submission in lesson practice. Final exams never reveal answer keys.

Hand hygiene performed correctly and at the right times

internal_challenge.rationale_internal: Hand hygiene is universally recognized as the single most effective infection prevention measure.

cards.module_title: Infection Control and PPE
cards.display_title: Environmental Cleaning and Safe Practices - Challenge Debrief
cards.learner_facing_content:
Challenge Debrief - lesson re-teaching extension. Your response has been submitted.

Safety Principle: Infection prevention starts with the action that breaks the chain of transmission for every resident, not just the ones who look sick. Hand hygiene, done correctly and at the right moments, is that action.

Safest Response: Hand hygiene performed correctly and at the right times
Why This Is Safest: Hand hygiene performed correctly and at the right times is the single most effective way to prevent the spread of infection in long-term care. Hands are the main way germs travel between residents, surfaces, and you, so clean hands before and after every contact stop transmission at its most common point.

CNA Scope Note: Performing hand hygiene at the right moments is squarely within CNA scope. You are expected to clean your hands before and after resident contact, after removing gloves, and after contact with body fluids or contaminated surfaces.
Resident Safety Note: Every resident is protected when you clean your hands at the right times. This is also how you protect yourself, your coworkers, and residents who cannot fight infection well.

What to Remember: Gloves, masks, and isolation all have a place, but none of them replace hand hygiene. Clean hands at the right moments are your strongest, most reliable infection-control tool on every shift.

remediation.safety_principle: Infection prevention starts with the action that breaks the chain of transmission for every resident, not just the ones who look sick. Hand hygiene, done correctly and at the right moments, is that action.

safest_response.text: Hand hygiene performed correctly and at the right times

remediation.why_correct: Hand hygiene performed correctly and at the right times is the single most effective way to prevent the spread of infection in long-term care. Hands are the main way germs travel between residents, surfaces, and you, so clean hands before and after every contact stop transmission at its most common point.

why_incorrect.A: Gloves are useful, but wearing them for all contact does not replace hand hygiene. Germs reach the skin when gloves are removed or reused, and gloves give a false sense of safety if hands are not cleaned before and after.
why_incorrect.C: A surgical mask protects against certain droplet exposures but does nothing about germs carried on the hands, which cause most transmission in long-term care. Routine masking for all care is not the most effective general measure.
why_incorrect.D: Isolating residents with known infections is only part of the picture. Many residents carry germs without obvious signs, so relying on isolation alone misses the everyday transmission that hand hygiene prevents.

option_rationales.label: Wearing gloves during all resident contact
option_rationales.why: Gloves are useful, but wearing them for all contact does not replace hand hygiene. Germs reach the skin when gloves are removed or reused, and gloves give a false sense of safety if hands are not cleaned before and after.
option_rationales.remember: Always perform hand hygiene before applying and after removing gloves - gloves are an addition to clean hands, not a substitute.

option_rationales.label: Hand hygiene performed correctly and at the right times
option_rationales.why: Hand hygiene performed correctly and at the right times is the single most effective measure for preventing the spread of infection, because hands are the most common way germs move between residents and surfaces.
option_rationales.remember: Make correct, well-timed hand hygiene your automatic first infection-control habit on every shift.

option_rationales.label: Using a surgical mask during all care
option_rationales.why: A surgical mask protects against certain droplet exposures but does nothing about germs carried on the hands, which cause most transmission in long-term care. Routine masking for all care is not the most effective general measure.
option_rationales.remember: Use masks when the situation or precautions call for them - they support, but never replace, hand hygiene.

option_rationales.label: Isolating all residents with known infections
option_rationales.why: Isolating residents with known infections is only part of the picture. Many residents carry germs without obvious signs, so relying on isolation alone misses the everyday transmission that hand hygiene prevents.
option_rationales.remember: Standard precautions and hand hygiene apply to every resident, not only those already known to be infected.

remediation.resident_safety_note: Every resident is protected when you clean your hands at the right times. This is also how you protect yourself, your coworkers, and residents who cannot fight infection well.
remediation.cna_scope_note: Performing hand hygiene at the right moments is squarely within CNA scope. You are expected to clean your hands before and after resident contact, after removing gloves, and after contact with body fluids or contaminated surfaces.
remediation.remember_this: Gloves, masks, and isolation all have a place, but none of them replace hand hygiene. Clean hands at the right moments are your strongest, most reliable infection-control tool on every shift.

modules.module_title: Resident Rights, Abuse Prevention, and Boundaries
learning_objectives: Upon completion of this module, the learner will be able to:
Define key terminology related to resident rights, abuse, neglect, and exploitation.
Identify and describe at least eight types of elder abuse, including physical, verbal, psychological, sexual, financial, neglect, abandonment, involuntary seclusion, and healthcare fraud.
Recognize signs and symptoms of abuse and neglect using objective observations.
Explain the CNA's role as a California mandated reporter and describe the reporting process.
Differentiate between negligence and intentional abuse.
Describe at least four abuse-prevention strategies in long-term care settings.
Identify professional boundaries and their importance in protecting residents and staff.

cards.module_title: Resident Rights, Abuse Prevention, and Boundaries
cards.learner_facing_content:
Every person living in a long-term care facility has legal rights protected by federal and state law. These rights exist because residents are vulnerable - they depend on others for daily care, and that dependence must never lead to loss of dignity or control.
Key rights include:
Privacy - The right to private conversations, mail, phone calls, and personal care behind closed doors.
Dignity - The right to be treated with respect at all times, regardless of condition or behavior.
Choice - The right to make decisions about care, activities, meals, and daily schedule.
Grievances - The right to file complaints without fear of retaliation.
Informed Consent - The right to understand and agree to treatments and procedures before they happen.
Freedom from Restraints - The right to be free from physical or chemical restraints used for staff convenience.
Confidentiality - The right to have personal and health information kept private (HIPAA).

cards.module_title: Resident Rights, Abuse Prevention, and Boundaries
cards.display_title: Why Rights Matter in Daily Care
cards.learner_facing_content:
Resident rights are not abstract legal concepts. They show up in every shift:
Knocking before entering = privacy
Offering two meal choices = choice
Explaining what you are about to do before doing it = informed consent
Closing the curtain during personal care = dignity
Never discussing a resident's condition in the hallway = confidentiality
As a CNA, you practice resident rights hundreds of times a day, often without thinking about it. This module will help you recognize those moments - and catch the ones you might miss.

cards.module_title: Resident Rights, Abuse Prevention, and Boundaries
cards.learner_facing_content:
Two important protections exist outside the facility:
Ombudsman: An independent advocate who investigates complaints, mediates disputes, and ensures resident rights are upheld. Every long-term care facility must post ombudsman contact information. Residents and families can contact the ombudsman confidentially.
Resident Council: A group of residents who meet to discuss concerns, make requests, and influence facility policies. Staff may attend only if invited. The council is resident-led and must be supported by the facility.

Resident Council: A group of residents who meet to discuss concerns, make requests, and influence facility policies. Staff may attend only if invited. The council is resident-led and must be supported by the facility.
cards.transcript_text: Two important protections exist outside the facility: Ombudsman: An independent advocate who investigates complaints, mediates disputes, and ensures resident rights are upheld. Every long-term care facility must post ombudsman contact information. Residents and families can contact the ombudsman confidentially. Resident Council: A group of residents who meet to discuss concerns, make requests, and influence facility policies. Staff may attend only if invited. The council is resident-led and must be supported by the facility.

cards.module_title: Resident Rights, Abuse Prevention, and Boundaries
cards.learner_facing_content:
Residents have the right to make decisions about their end-of-life care through advance directives:
Advance Directive: A legal document stating a person's wishes about medical treatment if they become unable to communicate.
Durable Power of Attorney for Health Care: Appoints someone to make medical decisions on the resident's behalf.
Do Not Resuscitate (DNR) / No Code: An order that CPR will not be performed if the resident's heart or breathing stops.
Your role as a CNA:
Know each resident's code status before an emergency occurs.
Never ignore a DNR order.
Report any questions about advance directives to the licensed nurse.
Never pressure a resident about their advance directive decisions.

cards.module_title: Resident Rights, Abuse Prevention, and Boundaries
cards.learner_facing_content:
Challenge Debrief - lesson re-teaching extension. Your response has been submitted.

Safety Principle: When you are unsure, choose the action that protects the resident first, stays inside CNA scope, and keeps required observe-report-document and care-plan steps in the right order.

Safest Response: Financial abuse
Why This Is Safest: Taking money from a resident without consent is financial abuse.

CNA Scope Note: As a CNA you observe, report, document, and follow the care plan. You do not diagnose, change orders, or perform tasks outside your training and assignment.
Resident Safety Note: Resident safety and dignity come first. Prevent avoidable harm, protect privacy, and report any change of condition promptly to the licensed nurse.

What to Remember: Lead with the action that keeps the resident safest, then complete the remaining observe-report-document steps in order. Taking money from a resident without consent is financial abuse.

cards.module_title: Resident Rights, Abuse Prevention, and Boundaries
cards.learner_facing_content:
Abuse is an intentional act - or failure to act - by a caregiver or other person in a relationship involving an expectation of trust, that causes or creates a risk of harm to a person age 60 or older.
Abuse is a crime. It includes one or more of the following:
Willful causing of injury
Unreasonable confinement
Intimidation - making someone afraid
Punishment
Refusal of goods or services needed for physical, mental, or psychological well-being
Abuse can happen to any older person, but it most often affects those who:
Depend on others for help with daily activities
Have no family or friends nearby
Have disabilities, memory problems, cognitive impairment, or dementia
cards.learning_goal: Define key terminology related to resident rights, abuse, neglect, and exploitation.

cards.module_title: Resident Rights, Abuse Prevention, and Boundaries
cards.learner_facing_content:
The following types of elder abuse are recognized under California law and federal regulations:
1. Verbal Abuse Teasing, profanity, racial slurs, or threats directed at a resident.
2. Physical Abuse Battery (kicking, biting, hitting, shoving, pulling hair) or rough handling during care.
3. Psychological / Emotional Abuse Ridiculing, saying hurtful words, repeatedly ignoring, manipulating, yelling at, or threatening a resident.
4. Involuntary Seclusion Isolating a resident or keeping them from seeing close friends and relatives against their will.
5. Abandonment Leaving a resident unattended or failing to plan for their care.
6. Neglect Failure to provide care that a reasonable person would provide. Examples: not answering the call light, leaving a resident smelling of urine without cleaning or changing them.
7. Sexual Abuse Sexual overtures, innuendos, inappropriate gestures, inappropriate touching, or forcing a resident to watch or participate in sexual acts.
8. Financial Abuse Stealing money, borrowing items, accepting gifts, forging checks, or using a resident's bank accounts, credit cards, or property without permission.
Also recognized: 9. Healthcare Fraud - Overcharging, double-billing, falsifying Medicare/Medicaid claims, or charging for care not provided. Committed by healthcare providers or facility staff.
cards.learning_goal: Define key terminology related to resident rights, abuse, neglect, and exploitation.

Preserves compliance boundaries: no PHI, no clinical-hour credit, certificate production disabled.

cards.module_title: Resident Rights, Abuse Prevention, and Boundaries
cards.learner_facing_content:
Recognizing examples in practice:
A CNA calls a resident a racial slur when no one else is in the room. - this is Verbal Abuse.
A staff member takes $20 from a resident's nightstand. - this is Financial Abuse.
A resident is left in a soiled brief for three hours despite pressing the call light. - this is Neglect.
A resident is told she cannot leave her room because "she causes too much trouble." - this is Involuntary Seclusion.
A CNA yanks a resident's arm during a transfer. - this is Physical Abuse.
A staff member repeatedly tells a resident, "Nobody cares about you." - this is Psychological Abuse.
cards.learning_goal: Define key terminology related to resident rights, abuse, neglect, and exploitation.
cards.cna_practice_example: Connect this screen to a routine CNA decision, then report concerns to the licensed nurse as appropriate.
why_it_matters: Supports safe CNA judgment within scope of practice.
Builds audit-ready completion evidence for required online theory only.
Preserves compliance boundaries: no PHI, no clinical-hour credit, certificate production disabled.

cards.module_title: Resident Rights, Abuse Prevention, and Boundaries
cards.learner_facing_content:
These terms sound similar but have different meanings:
Neglect: A pattern of failing to provide care - such as repeatedly not answering call lights, not feeding residents, or not changing soiled linens. Neglect can be intentional or the result of chronic understaffing.
Negligence: A single act or failure to act that a reasonably careful person would not have done (or would have done). Example: Forgetting to lock the wheelchair brakes before a transfer, resulting in a fall.
Both are serious. Both must be reported. But neglect typically involves a pattern, while negligence may be a one-time error.
Your responsibility: Report both. Do not try to determine which one it is - that is for the investigation to decide.

cards.display_title: Types of Abuse and Neglect Challenge
cards.learner_facing_content:
Scenario challenge for Types of Abuse and Neglect: As a California CNA you are a mandated reporter. This means you must report suspected abuse:

A) Only after you have collected evidence to prove it occurred
B) Only if you personally witnessed it
C) Immediately based on reasonable suspicion - certainty is not required
D) Only after your supervisor gives you permission

No answer is revealed until submission in lesson practice. Final exams never reveal answer keys.

choices.label: Only after you have collected evidence to prove it occurred

cards.module_title: Resident Rights, Abuse Prevention, and Boundaries
cards.display_title: Types of Abuse and Neglect - Challenge Debrief
cards.learner_facing_content:
Challenge Debrief - lesson re-teaching extension. Your response has been submitted.

Safety Principle: When you are unsure, choose the action that protects the resident first, stays inside CNA scope, and keeps required observe-report-document and care-plan steps in the right order.

Safest Response: Immediately based on reasonable suspicion - certainty is not required
Why This Is Safest: Mandated reporters must report on reasonable suspicion. Certainty and personal witness are not prerequisites.

CNA Scope Note: As a CNA you observe, report, document, and follow the care plan. You do not diagnose, change orders, or perform tasks outside your training and assignment.
Resident Safety Note: Resident safety and dignity come first. Prevent avoidable harm, protect privacy, and report any change of condition promptly to the licensed nurse.

What to Remember: Lead with the action that keeps the resident safest, then complete the remaining observe-report-document steps in order. Mandated reporters must report on reasonable suspicion. Certainty and personal witness are not prerequisites.

Builds audit-ready completion evidence for required online theory only.

The safest response is to immediately based on reasonable suspicion - certainty is not required. Mandated reporters must report on reasonable suspicion. Certainty and personal witness are not prerequisites. As a CNA, stay within scope: observe, report, document, and follow the care plan. Resident safety and dignity come first, so report any change of condition promptly to the nurse. Before you continue, review the safest response and your own choice so the reason stays with you on the floor.

cards.module_title: Resident Rights, Abuse Prevention, and Boundaries
cards.lesson_title: Signs, Symptoms, and Evidence of Abuse
cards.learner_facing_content:
You may not witness abuse directly. Instead, you will see its effects. Watch for:
Behavioral Signs:
New-onset sleeping problems
Depression or confusion that was not present before
Unexplained weight loss
Trauma signs: rocking back and forth, flinching at touch
Agitation or violent behavior (new or worsening)
Withdrawal from activities the resident previously enjoyed
Being overly anxious to please a caregiver
Physical Signs:
Unexplained bruises, burns, or scars
Appearing unkempt - unwashed hair, dirty clothes
Bed sores or other preventable conditions
Frequent injuries
Environmental Signs:
Living conditions that are unsafe, unclean, or inadequate
Personal hygiene that is consistently lacking
Medications not taken properly
Private conversations not allowed between the resident and visitors
cards.learning_goal: Identify and describe at least eight types of elder abuse, including physical, verbal, psychological, sexual, financial, neglect, abandonment, involuntary seclusion, and healthcare fraud.
cards.cna_practice_example: Connect this screen to a routine CNA decision, then report concerns to the licensed nurse as appropriate.
why_it_matters: Supports safe CNA judgment within scope of practice.
Builds audit-ready completion evidence for required online theory only.
Preserves compliance boundaries: no PHI, no clinical-hour credit, certificate production disabled.

cards.module_title: Resident Rights, Abuse Prevention, and Boundaries
cards.lesson_title: Signs, Symptoms, and Evidence of Abuse
cards.learner_facing_content:
When you observe potential abuse, you must document and report using objective observations:
Objective: What you can see, hear, feel, or measure. Examples: "Bruise approximately 3 cm on left forearm, purple-yellow in color." "Resident stated: 'She hit me.'"
Subjective: What you think or feel. Example: "I think someone is hurting her."
Report and document objective observations. Include the resident's exact words in quotes when possible. Do not include your opinions in the chart - but do share your concerns verbally with the licensed nurse.
Never document in a way that identifies a real person by full name, date of birth, or facility in training exercises. All training scenarios in this course use fictional examples only.

why_it_matters: Supports safe CNA judgment within scope of practice.
Builds audit-ready completion evidence for required online theory only.
Preserves compliance boundaries: no PHI, no clinical-hour credit, certificate production disabled.

cards.display_title: Signs, Symptoms, and Evidence of Abuse - Challenge Debrief
cards.learner_facing_content:
Challenge Debrief - lesson re-teaching extension. Your response has been submitted.

Safety Principle: When you are unsure, choose the action that protects the resident first, stays inside CNA scope, and keeps required observe-report-document and care-plan steps in the right order.

Safest Response: Abandonment
Why This Is Safest: Abandonment is defined as leaving a resident unattended or failing to plan for their care.

CNA Scope Note: As a CNA you observe, report, document, and follow the care plan. You do not diagnose, change orders, or perform tasks outside your training and assignment.
Resident Safety Note: Resident safety and dignity come first. Prevent avoidable harm, protect privacy, and report any change of condition promptly to the licensed nurse.

What to Remember: Lead with the action that keeps the resident safest, then complete the remaining observe-report-document steps in order. Abandonment is defined as leaving a resident unattended or failing to plan for their care.

cards.module_title: Resident Rights, Abuse Prevention, and Boundaries
cards.learner_facing_content:
In California, all CNAs are mandated reporters. This means:
You are legally required to report known or suspected abuse, neglect, or exploitation of an elder or dependent adult.
You must report immediately - first to your licensed nurse/supervisor, then follow facility policy for external reporting.
You do not need to prove abuse happened. You only need reasonable suspicion.
Failure to report is a crime. Mandated reporters who fail to report can face fines and criminal penalties.
You are protected by law. Reports made in good faith cannot be used against you by your employer.
What triggers a report:
You witness abuse or neglect.
A resident tells you about abuse.
You observe signs that suggest abuse may have occurred.
A coworker tells you about abuse.
What does NOT excuse you from reporting:
"I wasn't sure."
"I didn't want to get anyone in trouble."
"My supervisor said not to worry about it."
"It only happened once."
cards.learning_goal: Recognize signs and symptoms of abuse and neglect using objective observations.
cards.cna_practice_example: Connect this screen to a routine CNA decision, then report concerns to the licensed nurse as appropriate.
why_it_matters: Supports safe CNA judgment within scope of practice.
Builds audit-ready completion evidence for required online theory only.
Preserves compliance boundaries: no PHI, no clinical-hour credit, certificate production disabled.

cards.module_title: Resident Rights, Abuse Prevention, and Boundaries
cards.learner_facing_content:
Step 1: You observe or learn about possible abuse. Step 2: Ensure the resident is safe and not in immediate danger. If they are, call for help immediately. Step 3: Report to the licensed nurse or charge nurse on duty. Step 4: Document your objective observations. Step 5: Follow your facility's policy for completing an incident report or abuse reporting form (e.g., SOC 341). Step 6: If your supervisor does not act, you may report directly to Adult Protective Services (APS), the Long-Term Care Ombudsman, or the Bureau of Medi-Cal Fraud and Elder Abuse.
You are not responsible for investigating. You are responsible for reporting what you observed.

cards.learner_facing_content:
Consider this fictional situation: During your evening shift, you enter the room of a fictional resident, "Mr. Rivera" (age 78, mild dementia). You notice a large bruise on his left wrist that was not there at the start of your shift. Mr. Rivera says, "That aide grabbed me hard when I wouldn't get out of bed." He looks frightened.

What to remember: Your first priorities are resident safety, immediate reporting to the licensed nurse, and objective documentation. You are a mandated reporter - time matters.

Safety Principle: When you are unsure, choose the action that protects the resident first, stays inside CNA scope, and keeps required observe-report-document and care-plan steps in the right order.

Safest Response: Ensure resident safety then report to the licensed nurse immediately and document objective observations
Why This Is Safest: The CNA ensures safety then reports immediately and documents objectively. Investigating and waiting are inappropriate.

CNA Scope Note: As a CNA you observe, report, document, and follow the care plan. You do not diagnose, change orders, or perform tasks outside your training and assignment.
Resident Safety Note: Resident safety and dignity come first. Prevent avoidable harm, protect privacy, and report any change of condition promptly to the licensed nurse.

What to Remember: Lead with the action that keeps the resident safest, then complete the remaining observe-report-document steps in order. The CNA ensures safety then reports immediately and documents objectively. Investigating and waiting are inappropriate.

cards.module_title: Resident Rights, Abuse Prevention, and Boundaries
cards.learner_facing_content:
Abuse thrives in environments with poor oversight, high stress, and inadequate training. As a CNA, you can help prevent abuse by:
Recognizing your own stress triggers. Long shifts, difficult residents, and personal problems can lower your patience. Know when to ask for help.
Using effective monitoring systems. Answer call lights promptly. Check on residents regularly. Document what you observe.
Following policies and procedures. Know your facility's abuse-prevention policies and follow them consistently.
Encouraging family visits and social connections. Isolated residents are more vulnerable.
Speaking up early. If you see a coworker losing patience with a resident, say something before the situation escalates.
Taking care of yourself. Burnout and compassion fatigue are real risk factors for abuse. Use support resources your employer provides.

cards.module_title: Resident Rights, Abuse Prevention, and Boundaries
cards.learner_facing_content:
Professional boundaries protect both the resident and you:
Do not accept gifts from residents or their families.
Do not share personal information about your finances, relationships, or problems with residents.
Do not develop romantic or sexual relationships with residents. This is always abuse.
Do not use your position to gain financial, emotional, or social benefits from a resident.
Do maintain respectful, warm, professional relationships that support the resident's care plan.
Boundary violations are a form of abuse - even if the resident initiated the contact. Residents are in a vulnerable position, and you are in a position of trust.

cards.module_title: Resident Rights, Abuse Prevention, and Boundaries
cards.display_title: HIPAA and Confidentiality in Abuse Situations
cards.learner_facing_content:
Reporting abuse does not violate HIPAA. Mandated reporting is a legal obligation that overrides general confidentiality rules.
However:
Report only to authorized persons (licensed nurse, supervisor, APS, ombudsman).
Do not discuss the situation with coworkers who are not involved in the care.
Do not post anything about residents or incidents on social media - ever.
Document only in official facility records, not on personal devices.
7. MODULE 2 KNOWLEDGE CHECK QUIZ

Type: Multiple choice Stem: A fictional resident, "Mrs. Chen," tells you that a staff member yelled at her and called her stupid. What type of abuse is this?

cards.learner_facing_content:
Scenario challenge for Prevention, Boundaries, and Safety: Every resident in a long-term care facility has the right to:

A) Have their care decisions made only by their physician
B) Be free from physical or chemical restraints used for staff convenience
C) Have their personal mail opened and read by staff for safety
D) Share a room with any resident the facility assigns without discussion

No answer is revealed until submission in lesson practice. Final exams never reveal answer keys.

learning_objectives: Upon completion of this module, the learner will be able to:
Define dementia, Alzheimer's disease, delirium, and related cognitive conditions.
Describe at least five communication strategies for residents with cognitive impairment.
Identify common behavioral responses in dementia (agitation, sundowning, catastrophic reactions, elopement risk) and describe appropriate CNA responses.
Explain the importance of cultural sensitivity and respect for spiritual beliefs in daily care.
Describe the five stages of grief according to Kubler-Ross and apply them to resident and family interactions.
Identify the rights of the dying resident and comfort care measures.
Describe the CNA's role in supporting residents receiving hospice or palliative care.

cards.module_title: Dementia, Communication, and Respectful Care
cards.learner_facing_content:
Dementia is not a single disease. It is a group of symptoms affecting memory, thinking, and social abilities severely enough to interfere with daily life. Dementia involves damage to brain cells that affects communication between cells.
Key facts:
Dementia is not a normal part of aging. Many older adults never develop dementia.
Alzheimer's disease is the most common cause, accounting for 60-80% of cases.
Other causes include vascular dementia (after strokes), Lewy body dementia, and frontotemporal dementia.
Dementia is progressive - symptoms worsen over time.
There is currently no cure, but symptoms can sometimes be managed.
Important distinction - Delirium vs. Dementia:
Delirium is sudden confusion caused by illness, infection (such as UTI), medication changes, or dehydration. It is often reversible with treatment.
Dementia develops gradually and is not reversible.
If a resident who has been stable suddenly becomes confused, this may be delirium, not worsening dementia. Report any sudden change in mental status to the licensed nurse immediately.

why_it_matters: Supports safe CNA judgment within scope of practice.

cards.module_title: Dementia, Communication, and Respectful Care
cards.learner_facing_content:
Residents with dementia may display behaviors that are challenging but always have a reason - even if the reason is not immediately obvious. Common behavioral symptoms include:
Agitation: Restlessness, pacing, yelling, or refusing care. Often triggered by pain, overstimulation, unfamiliar environments, or unmet needs.
Sundowning (Sundowner Syndrome): Increased confusion, agitation, or anxiety in the late afternoon or evening. May be related to fatigue, low light, or disrupted internal clocks.
Catastrophic Reactions: Sudden, intense emotional responses (crying, screaming, striking out) triggered by frustration, confusion, or sensory overload.
Elopement: Leaving the facility without authorization. Residents with dementia may wander due to confusion, restlessness, or trying to go "home."
Hallucinations and Delusions: Seeing or hearing things that are not there (hallucinations) or believing things that are not true (delusions). Common in certain types of dementia.
Repetitive Behaviors: Asking the same question repeatedly, repeating motions. The resident genuinely does not remember asking.
Your response: Stay calm. Do not argue. Do not take it personally. The behavior is the disease, not the person.

cards.module_title: Dementia, Communication, and Respectful Care
cards.learner_facing_content:
Alzheimer's disease typically progresses through three general stages:
Early Stage:
Forgetfulness (especially recent events)
Difficulty finding words
Misplacing objects
Getting lost in familiar places
May still perform most ADLs independently
Middle Stage:
Increasing memory loss and confusion
Difficulty recognizing family and friends
Problems with dressing, bathing, and toileting
Behavioral changes: agitation, suspicion, wandering
May need assistance with most ADLs
Late Stage:
Severe memory loss - may not recognize self or loved ones
Loss of ability to communicate
Loss of ability to walk, sit, or swallow
Total dependence on caregivers for all ADLs
Increased vulnerability to infections (pneumonia, UTI)
Your role changes with each stage. In early stages, encourage independence. In middle stages, provide structured support. In late stages, focus on comfort, dignity, and safety.

cards.module_title: Dementia, Communication, and Respectful Care
cards.learner_facing_content:
Dementia is not the only condition that affects cognition. You may also care for residents with:
Parkinson's Disease: A progressive nervous system disorder causing tremors, stiffness, and slow movement. Dementia may develop in later stages.
Cerebral Vascular Accident (CVA / Stroke): May cause paralysis, speech difficulty (aphasia), and cognitive changes depending on which area of the brain is affected.
Intellectual Disability / Developmental Disability: Lifelong conditions present from birth or early childhood. Residents need individualized care plans.
Mental Illness: Conditions such as bipolar disorder, schizophrenia, depression, anxiety, or PTSD. These require specific communication approaches and may require medication management by licensed staff.
Epilepsy: A seizure disorder. Know each resident's seizure protocol and keep the environment safe.
For all conditions: Follow the individualized care plan. Report any changes. Treat every resident with dignity and respect.

cards.module_title: Dementia, Communication, and Respectful Care
cards.display_title: Understanding Dementia and Cognitive Conditions Challenge
cards.learner_facing_content:
Scenario challenge for Understanding Dementia and Cognitive Conditions: A resident with Alzheimer's disease repeatedly asks you what day it is even after you have answered. The BEST response is to:

A) Tell the resident to write it down so they remember
B) Remind the resident that you already told them
C) Answer patiently each time - the resident genuinely cannot retain the information
D) Ignore the question after the second time to discourage repetition

No answer is revealed until submission in lesson practice. Final exams never reveal answer keys.

Challenge Debrief - lesson re-teaching extension. Your response has been submitted.

Safety Principle: When you are unsure, choose the action that protects the resident first, stays inside CNA scope, and keeps required observe-report-document and care-plan steps in the right order.

Safest Response: Answer patiently each time - the resident genuinely cannot retain the information
Why This Is Safest: Repetitive questioning in dementia is caused by memory loss. Patient repetition is appropriate.

CNA Scope Note: As a CNA you observe, report, document, and follow the care plan. You do not diagnose, change orders, or perform tasks outside your training and assignment.
Resident Safety Note: Resident safety and dignity come first. Prevent avoidable harm, protect privacy, and report any change of condition promptly to the licensed nurse.

What to Remember: Lead with the action that keeps the resident safest, then complete the remaining observe-report-document steps in order. Repetitive questioning in dementia is caused by memory loss. Patient repetition is appropriate.

cards.module_title: Dementia, Communication, and Respectful Care
cards.learner_facing_content:
Effective communication with residents who have cognitive impairment requires you to adjust your approach:
Approach from the front. Make eye contact before speaking.
Use the resident's name. It helps orient them.
Speak slowly, clearly, and in short sentences. One idea at a time.
Use a calm, warm tone. Your tone communicates more than your words.
Give one-step directions. Instead of "Get up, go to the bathroom, and wash your hands," say "Let's stand up now."
Allow extra time for responses. Do not rush or interrupt.
Use nonverbal cues. Gentle touch, pointing, gestures, and facial expressions support understanding.
Avoid arguing or correcting. If a resident says it is 1965, do not argue. Redirect instead.
Validate emotions, not facts. "You miss your husband. That must be hard." - even if the husband has been gone for years.
Use reminiscence. Talking about past experiences can be comforting and meaningful.
cards.learning_goal: Define dementia, Alzheimer's disease, delirium, and related cognitive conditions.
cards.cna_practice_example: Connect this screen to a routine CNA decision, then report concerns to the licensed nurse as appropriate.
why_it_matters: Supports safe CNA judgment within scope of practice.
Builds audit-ready completion evidence for required online theory only.
Preserves compliance boundaries: no PHI, no clinical-hour credit, certificate production disabled.

cards.module_title: Dementia, Communication, and Respectful Care
cards.learner_facing_content:
The Best Friends Philosophy of Communication is a care approach that treats every interaction with a person with dementia as you would treat a conversation with a close friend:
Be present. Give your full attention.
Show genuine interest. Ask about their life, even if you have heard the story before.
Use humor gently. Laughter can ease tension and connect people.
Be flexible. If one approach is not working, try another.
Celebrate the person, not the disease. Focus on what they can do, not what they have lost.
Respect their history. Every person had a full life before this diagnosis.
This approach does not require extra time - it requires extra intention.

cards.learner_facing_content:

A) Your husband passed away ten years ago. He is not coming home.
B) You miss your husband. Tell me about him - what was he like?
C) You need to stop worrying. Everything is fine here.
D) I already told you - your husband is not coming.

cards.learner_facing_content:
Challenge Debrief - lesson re-teaching extension. Your response has been submitted.

Safety Principle: When you are unsure, choose the action that protects the resident first, stays inside CNA scope, and keeps required observe-report-document and care-plan steps in the right order.

Safest Response: You miss your husband. Tell me about him - what was he like?
Why This Is Safest: Validation acknowledges the emotion and redirects through reminiscence without arguing about facts.

CNA Scope Note: As a CNA you observe, report, document, and follow the care plan. You do not diagnose, change orders, or perform tasks outside your training and assignment.
Resident Safety Note: Resident safety and dignity come first. Prevent avoidable harm, protect privacy, and report any change of condition promptly to the licensed nurse.

What to Remember: Lead with the action that keeps the resident safest, then complete the remaining observe-report-document steps in order. Validation acknowledges the emotion and redirects through reminiscence without arguing about facts.
cards.learning_goal: Explain the decision and apply non-punitive remediation.
cards.cna_practice_example: After a missed practice decision, review the key safety step and repeat the scenario without penalty.
why_it_matters: Supports safe CNA judgment within scope of practice.
Builds audit-ready completion evidence for required online theory only.
Preserves compliance boundaries: no PHI, no clinical-hour credit, certificate production disabled.

key_terms.definition: The learner observes, reports, documents, and follows the care plan within allowed CNA duties.

The safest response is to you miss your husband. tell me about him - what was he like?. Validation acknowledges the emotion and redirects through reminiscence without arguing about facts. As a CNA, stay within scope: observe, report, document, and follow the care plan. Resident safety and dignity come first, so report any change of condition promptly to the nurse. cards.module_title: Dementia, Communication, and Respectful Care
cards.display_title: Cultural Sensitivity and Spiritual Respect Challenge
cards.learner_facing_content:
Scenario challenge for Cultural Sensitivity and Spiritual Respect: The five stages of grief identified by Elisabeth Kubler-Ross are:

A) Shock then sadness then anger then fear then recovery
B) Denial then anger then bargaining then depression then acceptance
C) Confusion then denial then guilt then mourning then closure
D) Fear then anger then surrender then peace then hope

No answer is revealed until submission in lesson practice. Final exams never reveal answer keys.

cards.module_title: Dementia, Communication, and Respectful Care
cards.display_title: End-of-Life Care, Grief, and Hospice Challenge
cards.learner_facing_content:
Scenario challenge for End-of-Life Care, Grief, and Hospice: Which of the following best describes hospice care?

A) Aggressive treatment aimed at curing the terminal illness
B) Comfort-focused care for individuals with a life expectancy of six months or less
C) Care provided only in a hospital setting
D) A program for residents who have refused all medical treatment

No answer is revealed until submission in lesson practice. Final exams never reveal answer keys.

cards.module_title: Dementia, Communication, and Respectful Care
cards.display_title: End-of-Life Care, Grief, and Hospice - Challenge Debrief
cards.learner_facing_content:
Challenge Debrief - lesson re-teaching extension. Your response has been submitted.

Safety Principle: When you are unsure, choose the action that protects the resident first, stays inside CNA scope, and keeps required observe-report-document and care-plan steps in the right order.

Safest Response: Comfort-focused care for individuals with a life expectancy of six months or less
Why This Is Safest: Hospice is comfort-focused care for those with a life expectancy of six months or less.

CNA Scope Note: As a CNA you observe, report, document, and follow the care plan. You do not diagnose, change orders, or perform tasks outside your training and assignment.
Resident Safety Note: Resident safety and dignity come first. Prevent avoidable harm, protect privacy, and report any change of condition promptly to the licensed nurse.

What to Remember: Lead with the action that keeps the resident safest, then complete the remaining observe-report-document steps in order. Hospice is comfort-focused care for those with a life expectancy of six months or less.

Builds audit-ready completion evidence for required online theory only.

cards.learner_facing_content:
Scenario challenge for Trauma-Informed Care and De-escalation: Sudden confusion in a resident who has been mentally stable is most likely a sign of:

A) Normal progression of dementia
B) Delirium possibly caused by infection or medication change or dehydration
C) A personality disorder
D) The resident being intentionally difficult

No answer is revealed until submission in lesson practice. Final exams never reveal answer keys.

learning_objectives: Upon completion of this module, the learner will be able to:
Describe and apply the principles of proper body mechanics for lifting, transferring, and repositioning.
Identify at least five fall risk factors and five fall prevention strategies.
Describe safe transfer techniques for bed-to-wheelchair, wheelchair-to-toilet, and sit-to-stand.
Explain active range of motion (AROM) and passive range of motion (PROM) and the CNA's role in each.
Describe the purpose and safe use of assistive/adaptive devices (gait belt, walker, cane, wheelchair, slide board).
Identify the CNA's role in restorative care programs and maintenance of functional ability.
Describe emergency response procedures for fire (RACE/PASS), choking (Heimlich maneuver), and disasters.

cards.module_title: Mobility, Falls, and Workplace Safety
cards.learner_facing_content:
Body mechanics is the use of correct muscle groups and body positions during activity to prevent injury. As a CNA, you lift, turn, reposition, and transfer residents many times per shift. Using poor body mechanics puts you at risk for serious back, shoulder, and knee injuries.
Core principles:
Maintain a wide base of support. Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, one foot slightly ahead of the other.
Bend at the knees and hips, not the waist. Use your strong leg muscles, not your back.
Keep the load close to your body. The farther you hold a weight from your center of gravity, the more strain on your back.
Tighten your core. Engage your abdominal muscles before lifting.
Pivot - do not twist. Move your feet to change direction. Twisting while carrying weight is the number one cause of back injury.
Push rather than pull when possible. Pushing uses larger muscle groups and is easier to control.
Know your limits. If a resident or object is too heavy, get help or use a mechanical lift.

cards.module_title: Mobility, Falls, and Workplace Safety
cards.learner_facing_content:
CNAs have one of the highest injury rates of any profession. Common injuries include:
Back injuries - from lifting, transferring, or repositioning without proper body mechanics.
Shoulder injuries - from reaching, pulling, or holding residents during transfers.
Slips, trips, and falls - from wet floors, cluttered hallways, or improper footwear.
Repetitive strain injuries - from repeated motions such as turning residents or making beds.
Needlestick injuries - from handling sharps improperly.
Exposure to body fluids - from splashes during care.
Prevention strategies:
Use proper body mechanics every time - not just when someone is watching.
Use mechanical lifts for residents who cannot bear weight.
Wear non-slip shoes with closed toes.
Report wet floors, broken equipment, and cluttered walkways immediately.
Follow Standard Precautions for infection control (reviewed in Module 1).

cards.learner_facing_content:

A) Directly in front of the resident
B) Slightly behind and to the side of the resident's stronger side
C) Slightly behind and to the side of the resident's weaker side
D) Several feet away to encourage independence

remediation.cna_scope_note: As a CNA you observe, report, document, and follow the care plan. You do not diagnose, change orders, or perform tasks outside your training and assignment.
remediation.remember_this: Lead with the action that keeps the resident safest, then complete the remaining observe-report-document steps in order. The CNA stands on the weaker side to support and prevent falls if balance is lost toward that side.

cards.module_title: Mobility, Falls, and Workplace Safety
cards.learner_facing_content:
Before any transfer, follow these steps:
Check the care plan. Know the resident's weight-bearing status, required assistance level, and any transfer restrictions.
Explain what you are doing. "Mr. Davis, I'm going to help you move from the bed to the wheelchair now."
Lock all wheels. Wheelchair brakes, bed brakes - everything must be locked.
Use a gait belt unless contraindicated. Place it snugly around the resident's waist over clothing, not over bare skin or a gown opening.
Clear the path. Remove obstacles, ensure adequate space.
Position yourself correctly. Feet shoulder-width apart, knees bent, close to the resident.
Count together. "On three, we'll stand. One, two, three."
Support, do not lift. Guide the resident's movement; encourage them to help as much as they are able.
cards.learning_goal: Describe and apply the principles of proper body mechanics for lifting, transferring, and repositioning.
cards.cna_practice_example: Connect this screen to a routine CNA decision, then report concerns to the licensed nurse as appropriate.
why_it_matters: Supports safe CNA judgment within scope of practice.

cards.module_title: Mobility, Falls, and Workplace Safety
cards.learner_facing_content:
Bed to Wheelchair:
Raise the bed to a comfortable working height if adjustable.
Lower the side rail on the transfer side.
Assist the resident to sit on the edge of the bed. Allow them to sit for a moment to prevent dizziness (orthostatic hypotension).
Place the wheelchair at a 45-degree angle to the bed, on the resident's strong side. Lock the brakes. Move footrests out of the way.
Apply the gait belt. Stand facing the resident.
On the count of three, help the resident stand using their legs.
Pivot (do not twist) and lower the resident into the wheelchair.
Position footrests. Ensure alignment and comfort.
Stand-Pivot Transfer: Used when the resident can bear weight on at least one leg. Follow the same steps as bed-to-wheelchair, with the CNA providing stability during the pivot.
Slide Board Transfer: Used when the resident cannot bear weight. A smooth board is placed as a bridge between the bed and wheelchair. The resident slides across with assistance. Requires specific training.
Mechanical Lift: Required when the resident cannot bear weight and is too heavy for manual transfer. Follow manufacturer instructions and facility policy. Always use two staff members.
cards.learning_goal: Describe and apply the principles of proper body mechanics for lifting, transferring, and repositioning.

cards.module_title: Mobility, Falls, and Workplace Safety
cards.learner_facing_content:
Ambulation means walking. Many residents need assistance to walk safely.
Assisting with ambulation:
Check the care plan for assistance level and equipment needed.
Ensure the resident is wearing non-skid footwear.
Apply a gait belt.
Stand slightly behind and to the side of the resident's weaker side.
Walk at the resident's pace - never rush.
Encourage the resident to look forward, not down at their feet.
If the resident becomes dizzy or unsteady, assist them to the nearest safe seat.
Using a walker:
Ensure all four legs or wheels touch the ground.
The resident should stand inside the walker, not behind it.
The walker moves first, then the weaker leg, then the stronger leg.
Using a cane:
The cane is held on the strong side.
The cane and the weak leg move forward together.
Then the strong leg steps forward.
cards.learning_goal: Describe and apply the principles of proper body mechanics for lifting, transferring, and repositioning.
cards.cna_practice_example: Connect this screen to a routine CNA decision, then report concerns to the licensed nurse as appropriate.
why_it_matters: Supports safe CNA judgment within scope of practice.
Builds audit-ready completion evidence for required online theory only.
Preserves compliance boundaries: no PHI, no clinical-hour credit, certificate production disabled.

Encourage the resident to look forward, not down at their feet. If the resident becomes dizzy or unsteady, assist them to the nearest safe seat. Using a walker: Ensure all four legs or wheels touch the ground. The resident should stand inside the walker, not behind it. The walker moves first, then the weaker leg, then the stronger leg. Using a cane: The cane is held on the strong side. The cane and the weak leg move forward together. Then the strong leg steps forward.

cards.module_title: Mobility, Falls, and Workplace Safety
cards.learner_facing_content:
Consider this fictional situation: You are assigned to transfer "Mr. Reeves" (age 85, 220 pounds, left-side weakness after stroke) from his bed to a wheelchair. The care plan says "stand-pivot transfer with gait belt, two-person assist." Your coworker is on break and no one else is available right now.

What to remember: The care plan requires two-person assist. Attempting a two-person transfer alone puts both you and the resident at risk. Wait for help.

cards.module_title: Mobility, Falls, and Workplace Safety
cards.learner_facing_content:
Scenario challenge for Safe Transfers and Ambulation: Before performing passive range of motion (PROM) exercises the CNA should:

A) Force the joint through the full range to maximize effectiveness
B) Only exercise the resident's stronger limbs
C) Check the care plan and support the limb above and below the joint being exercised
D) Ask the resident to perform the movements independently first

No answer is revealed until submission in lesson practice. Final exams never reveal answer keys.

cards.module_title: Mobility, Falls, and Workplace Safety
cards.lesson_title: Range of Motion and Restorative Care
cards.learner_facing_content:
Range of motion (ROM) refers to the full extent to which a joint can move. When residents are immobile or inactive, their joints can become stiff and their muscles can shorten - a condition called contracture.
Three types of ROM exercises:
Active ROM (AROM): The resident performs the exercises independently. The CNA may provide verbal cues and encouragement.
Active-Assistive ROM (AAROM): The resident performs the exercises with some physical help from the CNA.
Passive ROM (PROM): The CNA moves the resident's joints through the range of motion. Used when the resident cannot move the joint independently.
Key rules for all ROM exercises:
Follow the care plan. Know which joints and movements are ordered.
Support the limb above and below the joint being exercised.
Move gently and smoothly - never force a joint past the point of resistance or pain.
Watch the resident's face for signs of pain.
Perform exercises in a systematic order (head to toe, or proximal to distal).
Report any new pain, swelling, or decreased range to the licensed nurse.
cards.learning_goal: Identify at least five fall risk factors and five fall prevention strategies.
cards.cna_practice_example: Connect this screen to a routine CNA decision, then report concerns to the licensed nurse as appropriate.
why_it_matters: Supports safe CNA judgment within scope of practice.
Builds audit-ready completion evidence for required online theory only.
Preserves compliance boundaries: no PHI, no clinical-hour credit, certificate production disabled.
